nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater streams, forest creeks, and floodplain pools with dense leaf litter, submerged roots, and overhanging riparian vegetation; typically shallow, heavily shaded, and extremely soft and acidic

decorPreferences: deep layer of dried leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) to acidify water and provide spawning sites
dense planting — Java fern, Anubias, Cryptocoryne, mosses
multiple caves and coconut-shell halves for female spawning and refuge
driftwood and twisted roots to break sightlines
dark substrate (fine sand or black sand) to encourage natural coloration
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce surface light
peat filtration or blackwater extract to replicate biotope conditions

diet.notes: Insectivore in the wild, feeding primarily on small invertebrates, microcrustaceans, insect larvae, worms, and zooplankton. In the aquarium, accepts live and frozen foods readily: baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, Moina, micro-worms, blackworms, and frozen bloodworms are all taken eagerly. High-quality small pellets and micro-granules formulated for carnivorous dwarf cichlids can supplement but should not be the sole diet. Varied feeding 2–3 times daily is recommended.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, cardinal tetras kept in schools)
freshwater shrimp (in very large tanks with heavy cover, caveat: fry may be eaten)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species (territory and hybridisation risk)
aggressive or boisterous species that stress the pair
very fast surface swimmers that dominate feeding
eartheater cichlids and other bottom-dwellers competing for territory

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Britzke, 4006x2520. Sharp, vibrant mature male: greenish-gold body, black horizontal lateral stripe, and pronounced lyrate caudal fin extensions all clearly shown, whole fish in frame.
Photo by Britzke, 4024x2532. Crisp lateral view emphasising the two prominent black stripes and yellow belly with extended pelvic fins; different individual for variety.
Photo by Britzke, ~1024px. Distinct sharp male showing gold flank, lateral stripe, blue spangling and lyrate tail; adds a third angle.

summary: The Two-Striped Dwarf Cichlid (Apistogramma bitaeniata) is a sexually dimorphic dwarf cichlid from the blackwater streams of Peru and Brazil, prized for the pronounced lyrate tail extensions males develop at maturity and for its complex cave-spawning behaviour. It belongs to the pertensis species group, and wild locality forms from Río Tahuayo, Tefé, and Ucayali show notable colour variation.
careNotes:
Apistogramma bitaeniata belongs to the pertensis species group and is one of the more demanding Apistogramma species to keep long-term, primarily due to its strict water-chemistry requirements. Wild fish are collected from extremely soft, acidic blackwaters — pH as low as 4.0 and near-zero hardness — and while captive-bred specimens show greater tolerance, the species consistently thrives and colours up best in soft (gH 0–4), acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) water. Reverse-osmosis water remineralised to low hardness, supplemented with Indian almond leaves or peat filtration, closely replicates natural conditions. Temperature should be maintained between 25–28 °C; this species is intolerant of prolonged cold.

Multiple wild locality forms exist — Rio Tahuayo, Tefé (Brazil), and Ucayali (Peru) forms are the most frequently referenced in the hobby, and colour pattern, lyrate tail development in males, and maximum size can differ significantly between them. Keeping locality forms pure and avoiding hybridisation between forms is strongly encouraged by serious hobbyists.

Tank layout is critical: provide multiple caves (coconut shells, ceramic tubes), deep leaf litter, and abundant planting to create visual breaks and allow females a defended territory around the spawn site. The species is haremic in the wild, with a dominant male holding a territory encompassing several female sub-territories; in the aquarium a 1 male to 2–3 female harem group can be kept in a suitably large (120 L+) species tank or species-dominated community. Single pairs can be kept from 80 L. Two males in the same tank will fight unless the tank is very large with solid visual barriers.

Male A. bitaeniata are notably striking: body base coloration is yellow to greenish-gold with two prominent horizontal black stripes (the 'bitaeniata' in the name), and mature males develop pronounced lyrate extensions to the upper and lower lobes of the caudal fin — a key identification feature distinguishing them from other Apistogramma. Females are smaller, yellow, and take on intensified golden coloration when guarding eggs or fry.
breedingNotes: Apistogramma bitaeniata is a cave-spawner. The female selects a cave or hollow (coconut shell halves, upturned flowerpots, and ceramic tubes are all accepted) and lays a clutch of 30–80 red to orange adhesive eggs on the ceiling or wall inside. The male fertilises the spawn and is then typically excluded from the cave by the female, who provides all direct brood care. The female fans and guards the eggs, which hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 26–27 °C. Free-swimming fry emerge 4–6 days after hatching and can accept newly hatched brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), micro-worms, and finely powdered fry food from the outset. The female remains highly protective, actively shepherding the fry as a mobile school, and can be aggressive toward tankmates — including the male — during this period; it is advisable to remove the male or ensure the tank is large enough for him to avoid the female’s zone. Water quality must be pristine during rearing; small, frequent water changes with temperature- and pH-matched soft water are essential. Fry are sensitive to pH swings. Raising the temperature to 27–28 °C slightly accelerates development. Brood sizes of 40–60 are typical in aquaria. The species is generally considered moderately difficult to breed consistently due to exacting water-chemistry demands.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma bitaeniata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Peru, Brazil — upper Amazon basin, Río Tahuayo, Río Ucayali, Tefé region), water parameters (pH 4.0–6.5, temp 24–29°C, hardness 0–54 ppm/0–3 dH), adult size (males to 8 cm, females to 4 cm), diet (microinvertebrates, small frozen/live foods), breeding (cave-spawner, female broods, 30–80 eggs), pertensis-group classification, and lyrate tail description for males.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma bitaeniata
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Peru, Brazil), adult size (males to 8 cm TL), temperature range (24–29°C), and habitat description (blackwater streams). Confirmed carnivore/insectivore diet classification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Dwarf Cichlid Care
additional.notes: General Apistogramma and dwarf cichlid care guidance. Confirmed soft acidic water requirements, cave-spawning behaviour, recommended tank size (20+ gal / ~75 L minimum), diet of live and frozen small invertebrates, and intermediate care rating for bitaeniata-type Apistos.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma species guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source for pertensis-group classification, locality form variation (Rio Tahuayo, Tefé, Ucayali), haremic social structure, and breeding behaviour. Confirmed lyrate tail as key male identification feature. Noted hobbyist caution regarding hybridisation between locality forms.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Apistogramma genus overview
additional.notes: Supplementary background on Apistogramma genus ecology, species-group structure, and general husbandry. Cross-referenced pertensis-group placement of A. bitaeniata and blackwater biotope requirements.

fish.breedingNotes: The Two-Striped Dwarf Cichlid is a cave-spawner in which the female selects a cave and provides all direct brood care while the male is typically excluded from the site. Clutches range from 30 to 80 eggs. At 26–27 °C, eggs hatch in approximately 48 to 72 hours, with fry becoming free-swimming around 4 to 6 days later. Soft, acidic water (pH 4.5–6.0, gH 0–4) is required throughout. The female guards fry aggressively and the male should be separated or the tank must be large enough to keep him out of her territory. Fry accept newly hatched brine shrimp and micro-worms from first feeding.
